learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Prefix Sum, Difference Array, Loops, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine you are driving a taxi that only travels in one direction. At different locations, passengers enter and leave the taxi. Instead of simulating every passenger individually, you simply record how many passengers enter and leave at each stop, then calculate the running passenger count.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine you are driving a taxi that only travels in one direction. At different locations, passengers enter and leave the taxi. Instead of simulating every passenger individually, you simply record how many passengers enter and leave at each stop, then calculate the running passenger count.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use the Difference Array pattern whenever multiple range updates are followed by sequential queries over the entire range.
learning.signals: Whenever multiple range updates are followed by sequential queries, think of a Difference Array with Prefix Sum.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: There is a car with a fixed number of empty seats represented by capacity. The vehicle only drives east (it never turns around). You are given an array trips where trips[i] = [numPassengers, from, to] indicates that numPassengers passengers are picked up at location from and dropped off before location to. Return true if it is possible to complete all trips without exceeding the vehicle's capacity at any time, otherwise return false.

examples.explanation: At location 3, there are 5 passengers in the car, exceeding the capacity.
The maximum number of passengers at any point is exactly 5.

edgeCases: Trips starting at the same location
Trips ending at the same location

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: For every trip, visit every location from pickup to drop-off and update the passenger count. If the capacity is exceeded, return false.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Instead of checking every trip against every other trip, record only the changes at each location. Add passengers where they enter and subtract them where they leave. A running prefix sum tells you exactly how many passengers are in the car at every point.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Only passenger changes matter—not every location inside a trip.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever multiple range updates are followed by sequential queries, think of a Difference Array with Prefix Sum.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Each trip contributes only two updates regardless of its length. The prefix sum reconstructs the passenger count at every location in linear time.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever multiple range updates are followed by sequential queries, think of a Difference Array with Prefix Sum.
commonWrongPaths: Subtracting passengers after the destination instead of at the destination.
Using the trip length instead of passenger count.
Checking capacity before updating the prefix.

hints: Think about passenger changes rather than active trips.
commonMisconceptions: Subtracting passengers after the destination instead of at the destination.
Using the trip length instead of passenger count.
Checking capacity before updating the prefix.

pattern.name: Difference Array
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever multiple range updates are followed by sequential queries, think of a Difference Array with Prefix Sum.

approaches.title: Simulate Every Location
approaches.idea: For every trip, visit every location from pickup to drop-off and update the passenger count. If the capacity is exceeded, return false.
algorithm: Create an array representing every location.
For each trip, increment passengers at every location between from and to.
Check if any location exceeds capacity.
approaches.dryRun:
Trip [2,1,5]
Locations 1-4 +=2
Trip [3,3,7]
Locations 3-6 +=3
Location 3 has 5 passengers.

code.java: Brute force simulation omitted because it repeatedly updates every location.

approaches.title: Sorting Pickup and Drop Events
approaches.idea: Separate pickup and drop-off events, sort them, and simulate passenger movement in chronological order.

code.python: Uses sorted pickup and drop events.

approaches.title: Difference Array + Prefix Sum
approaches.idea: Create a difference array representing passenger changes at every location. Add passengers at the pickup location and subtract them at the drop-off location. Compute the running prefix sum to determine the current number of passengers.
approaches.intuition: Each trip contributes only two updates regardless of its length. The prefix sum reconstructs the passenger count at every location in linear time.
algorithm: Create a difference array of size 1001.
If passengers exceed capacity, return false.

visualization.description: Instead of tracking passengers continuously, track only where they enter and leave.

interviewFollowUps: Can this be solved without using a fixed-size array?
How would you solve it if locations reached 10^9?
Can a priority queue solve this problem?
What if the car could travel in both directions?
How does this relate to Range Addition?
interviewTips: Explain why updating only two indices is enough.
Mention that prefix sum reconstructs passenger counts.
Mention that the fixed array size is possible because locations are bounded.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Only passenger changes matter—not every location inside a trip.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Difference Array → Prefix Sum → Capacity Check.

commonMistakes: Subtracting passengers after the destination instead of at the destination.
Using the trip length instead of passenger count.
Checking capacity before updating the prefix.
